verbatim: For you did all things without respect of persons, and walked in the laws of God, obedient to your rulers, and paying all fitting honour to the older among you.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes the established social and religious conduct of the Corinthian church, noting that a specific resource (honor) is a required due for the group defined by the criterion of Age.

verbatim: On the young, too, you enjoined temperate and seemly thoughts, and to the women you gave instruction that they should do all things with a blameless and seemly and pure conscience, yielding a dutiful affection to their husbands.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines the communal rules applied to the youth within the church, where they are specifically targeted for moral guidance and the expectation of temperance.

verbatim: and to the women you gave instruction that they should do all things with a blameless and seemly and pure conscience, yielding a dutiful affection to their husbands. And you taught them to remain in the rule of obedience and to manage their households with seemliness, in all circumspection.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author explicitly identifies women as a group subject to specific rules regarding domestic management and marital obedience within the contemporary community structure.

verbatim: and to the women you gave instruction that they should do all things with a blameless and seemly and pure conscience, yielding a dutiful affection to their husbands. And you taught them to remain in the rule of obedience and to manage their households with seemliness, in all circumspection.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The 'rule of obedience' and the instruction to remain managing the household implies a restriction on the choice of life conduct and movement outside of the marital/domestic sphere.

verbatim: Wash you, and make you clean, put away your wickedness from your souls before my eyes, cease from your wickedness, learn to do good, seek out judgment, rescue the wronged, give judgment for the orphan, do justice to the widow
extracted_rules.reasoning: While quoting scripture, the author presents these as active 'ordinances' and the 'rule of tradition' that the contemporary community must follow, establishing protection for vulnerable groups as a social rule.

verbatim: learn to do good, seek out judgment, rescue the wronged, give judgment for the orphan, do justice to the widow, and come and let us reason together, saith the Lord
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement includes the requirement to provide judgment for orphans as part of the 'venerable rule' that the contemporary church is commanded to observe.

verbatim: learn to do good, seek out judgment, rescue the wronged, give judgment for the orphan, do justice to the widow, and come and let us reason together, saith the Lord
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies widows as a specific group entitled to 'justice' under the moral and communal laws being exhorted by the author.

verbatim: Let us reverence the Lord Jesus Christ, whose blood was given for us, let us respect those who rule us, let us honour the aged, let us instruct the young in the fear of God, let us lead our wives to that which is good.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement lists specific social hierarchies within the Christian community at Rome/Corinth, explicitly enjoining the 'honouring' of those defined by the criterion of age (the aged) as a communal rule.

verbatim: let us honour the aged, let us instruct the young in the fear of God, let us lead our wives to that which is good. Let our children share in the instruction which is in Christ, let them learn the strength of humility before God, the power of pure love before God, how beautiful and great is his fear and how it gives salvation to all who live holily in it with a pure mind.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a social rule where children (defined by age) are granted the resource of formal community instruction ('instruction in Christ') and religious education.

verbatim: let us instruct the young in the fear of God, let us lead our wives to that which is good. Let them exhibit the lovely habit of purity, let them show forth the innocent will of meekness, let them make the gentleness of their tongue manifest by their silence, let them not give their affection by factious preference, but in holiness to all equally who fear God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule where women (specifically wives) are 'led' by men and are expected to adhere to specific behavioral constraints like 'silence' and 'purity' under male guidance, indicating less autonomy in their conduct.

verbatim: Let us reverence the Lord Jesus Christ, whose blood was given for us, let us respect those who rule us, let us honour the aged, let us instruct the young in the fear of God, let us lead our wives to that which is good.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement articulates a clear rule of social order within the church where those in positions of leadership (rulers/presbyters) are entitled to the resource of 'respect' from the group.

verbatim: For the misery of the poor and groaning of the needy, now will I arise, saith the Lord, I will place him in safety, I will deal boldly with him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While quoting scripture, the author uses this to define the community's ethical stance: the poor (defined by wealth) are titled to safety and divine protection against those who 'magnify' themselves.

verbatim: The good workman receives the bread of his labour with boldness; the lazy and careless cannot look his employer in the face. Therefore we must be prompt in well-doing: for all things are from him. For he warns us: Behold the Lord cometh, and his reward is before his face, to pay to each according to his work.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement uses the contemporary social reality of the 'good workman' (laborer) receiving 'bread' (wages/sustenance) from an 'employer' as a metaphor for divine reward, confirming the established norm that labor entitles the worker to compensation.

verbatim: For from him come the priests and all the Levites, who serve the altar of God, from him comes the Lord Jesus according to the flesh, from him come the kings and rulers and governors in the succession of Judah, and the other sceptres of his tribes are in no small renown seeing that God promised that thy seed shall be as the stars of heaven.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the historical and religious rule of the Jewish tradition (which Clement and his audience recognize as a foundational precedent) where specific lineages (Levites/Judah) were granted exclusive access to specific political and religious resources (priesthood/kingship).

verbatim: For God, he says, resisteth the proud but giveth grace to the humble. Let us then join ourselves to those to whom is given grace from God; let us put on concord in meekness of spirit and continence, keeping ourselves far from all gossip and evil speaking, and be justified by deeds, not by words.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author cites a known rule of the community (scriptural in origin but applied as a social/religious norm) where social standing and divine favor are distributed based on the criterion of humility versus pride.

verbatim: For to the High Priest his proper ministrations are allotted, and to the priests the proper place has been appointed, and on Levites their proper services have been imposed. The layman is bound by the ordinances for the laity.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement describes a structured hierarchy of religious roles where the High Priest, priests, and Levites have exclusive rights to perform specific liturgical functions, contrasting them with the laity.

verbatim: For to the High Priest his proper ministrations are allotted, and to the priests the proper place has been appointed, and on Levites their proper services have been imposed. The layman is bound by the ordinances for the laity.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly limits the layman to a specific set of rules (ordinances for the laity), implying exclusion from the specific duties allotted to the high priests and Levites.

verbatim: Not in every place, my brethren, are the daily sacrifices offered or the free-will offerings, or the sin-offerings and trespass-offerings, but only in Jerusalem; and there also the offering is not made in every place, but before the shrine, at the altar, and the offering is first inspected by the High Priest and the ministers already mentioned. Those therefore who do anything contrary to that which is agreeable to his will suffer the penalty of death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement defines a strict rule regarding where and how sacrifices are to be conducted, noting that even for priests, offering sacrifices outside the designated place (Jerusalem) or without inspection is a capital offense.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to permanent tenure in office
verbatim: We consider therefore that it is not just to remove from their ministry those who were appointed by them, or later on by other eminent men, with the consent of the whole Church, and have ministered to the flock of Christ without blame, humbly, peaceably, and disinterestedly, and for many years have received a universally favourable testimony.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author argues that validly appointed bishops who have served blamelessly cannot be justly removed from their office, establishing a rule of tenure based on apostolic succession.

verbatim: For our sin is not small, if we eject from the episcopate those who have blamelessly and liolily offered its sacrifices.
For we see that in spite of their good service you have removed some from the ministry which they fulfilled blamelessly.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement rebukes the Corinthians for removing bishops, stating that it is a 'sin' to eject those who have performed their duties blamelessly, which implies an established norm protecting officers from removal without cause.

verbatim: If sedition and strife and divisions have arisen on my account, I will depart, I will go away whithersoever you will, and I will obey the commands of the people; only let the flock of Christ have peace with the presbyters set over it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes an established hierarchical order where 'presbyters' are 'set over' the 'flock' (the community). Clement presents this as a normative rule that should not be disrupted by sedition.

verbatim: We know that many among ourselves have given themselves to bondage that they might ransom others. Many have delivered themselves to slavery, and provided food for others with the price they received for themselves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement cites contemporary practices within his own community where individuals relinquish their freedom (selling themselves into slavery) to acquire resources (food or ransom) for other members.

verbatim: Give concord and peace to us and to all that dwell on the earth, as thou didst give to our fathers who called on thee in holiness with faith and truth, and grant that we may be obedient to thy almighty and glorious name, and to our rulers and governors upon the earth.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement explicitly anchors the Christian life in obedience to 'rulers and governors upon the earth,' establishing a rule of civil submission to external political authorities.

verbatim: You therefore, who laid the foundation of the sedition, submit to the presbyters, and receive the correction of repentance, bending the knees of your hearts. Learn to be submissive, putting aside the boastful and the haughty self-confidence of your tongue.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This refers to the specific contemporary conflict in Corinth. The rule identifies that those who challenge the established order (the seditious) must yield their autonomy and status to the presbyters.

verbatim: Thou, Master, hast given the power of sovereignty to them through thy excellent and inexpressible might, that we may know the glory and honour given to them by thee, and be subject to them, in nothing resisting thy will.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Clement explicitly identifies political rulers as having received sovereignty from God, and establishes a rule of subjection for the governed, defining the ruler's access to power and the subject's duty of non-resistance.

verbatim: For thou, heavenly Master, king of eternity, hast given to the sons of men glory and honour and power over the things which are on the earth; do thou, O Lord, direct their counsels according to that which is good and pleasing before thee.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a general rule governing the human species ('the sons of men'), granting them the resource of authority or 'power' over the material world.

_perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
